title: Drawing Line Types, Scales & Visual Conventions

data.overview: Understanding line types, weights, and conventions is essential for correctly identifying services on M&E drawings. A solid line means something different from a dashed line, and line weight indicates the importance/hierarchy of the element.

typical_meaning: Services above or below the plan cut level
Pipework in ceiling void (shown on floor plan)
dashed.tag: [INDICATED ON OTHER LEVEL] — verify which floor this service is actually on

chain_line.note: Do not measure along chain lines — they are reference lines, not services.

chain_double_dash.note: If this represents existing services, do NOT include in new work take-off.

dotted.measure: Only if confirmed as new work within scope

standard_scales.1_to_100: Floor plans — MOST COMMON for M&E GAs. 1cm on drawing = 1m actual.
standard_scales.1_to_50: Detailed floor plans, plant rooms. 2cm on drawing = 1m actual.
standard_scales.1_to_25: Plant room details, risers, complex areas

scale_bar_reading.rule: ALWAYS verify the scale bar before measuring. The scale bar is the ONLY reliable scale reference on a PDF.
process: 1. Find the scale bar (usually near title block or at drawing edge).
2. Measure the scale bar's physical length in the PDF viewer (many viewers have a measurement tool).
3. The bar should show divisions representing known distances (e.g., 0-1m-2m-5m-10m).
4. If the bar's divisions measure correctly against the stated scale, the drawing is at correct print scale.
5. If the bar's divisions DO NOT match the stated scale, the drawing has been printed/scaled. Use the BAR, not the stated scale.

common_causes: PDF printed at 'fit to page' instead of 100%
A3 drawing printed on A4 (everything halved)
PDF exported at different paper size than drawn
scale_error_detection.detection_method: Measure a known object: door (900mm leaf), structural grid (annotated spacing), corridor width (1200-1800mm typical), ceiling tile (600×600mm).
scale_error_detection.correction: If measured object is exactly 2× or 0.5× expected, the entire drawing is at double or half scale. Apply correction factor to ALL measurements.

colour_conventions.note: Colour use varies between projects. There is no universal UK standard for M&E service colours on drawings. ALWAYS check the legend.
colour_conventions.warning: Do NOT rely on colour for identification if no legend confirms the convention. Many drawings are printed in greyscale.

size_annotations.pipework: Number near the line = nominal bore in mm (e.g., '54' means 54mm NB). Sometimes prefixed with 'DN' (e.g., 'DN50').
size_annotations.ductwork_rectangular: Width × Height in mm (e.g., '600×400'). Width first, height second.
size_annotations.ductwork_circular: Ø followed by diameter (e.g., 'Ø315' means 315mm diameter).
size_annotations.containment: Width in mm (e.g., '300CT' = 300mm cable tray, '225CB' = 225mm cable basket).

level_annotations.ffl: Finished Floor Level (e.g., '+3.750' means 3.75m above datum)

reference_annotations.system_labels: System identification (e.g., 'LTHW-F' = LTHW Flow, 'SA-01' = Supply Air system 01)
reference_annotations.revision_clouds: Irregular cloud shape enclosing changed areas — triangle with revision letter nearby

drawing_reading_sequence.rule: When presented with an M&E drawing, read it in this order:
steps: 1. TITLE BLOCK — drawing number, title, revision, scale, status, discipline
2. LEGEND/KEY — index every symbol before measuring
3. NORTH POINT — orient yourself to the building
4. GRID LINES — note column grid references for location tracking
5. BUILDING OUTLINE — understand the floor plate, walls, openings
6. SERVICES OVERVIEW — scan for all service types present (what systems are on this drawing?)
7. SYSTEM-BY-SYSTEM TRACE — methodically trace each system from source to terminal
8. ANNOTATIONS — read all text annotations, dimension notes, flags
9. REVISION CLOUDS — identify what has changed if this is a revised drawing
10. NOTES — read general notes and specific notes panels (often contain critical scope information)
